steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis/biliary colic, hepatic cyst complication (hemorrhage or infection), nephrolithiasis/urologic source, gynecologic pathology (fibroid degeneration), bowel obstruction
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the RUQ pain remain undefined — no prior imaging has been performed, and the labs do not clearly point to a single etiology; the physician needs cross-sectional anatomy to identify the responsible structure.
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or cholelithiasis consistent with cholecystitis/biliary colic; alternatively, a complicated hepatic cyst, ureteral stone, or colonic/diverticular pathology given the known diverticulosis and uterine fibroids
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With RUQ pain of 7 days, tachycardia, leukocytosis (WBC 15.1), no prior imaging, and multiple competing diagnoses spanning biliary, hepatic, urologic, gynecologic, and colonic etiologies, a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the appropriate single test to survey all relevant structures simultaneously.
grounding: History: '7 days of cramping abdominal pain localized to the RUQ and nausea and emesis'
History: 'pain worsen and lasted longer then 4 hrs, hence she presented to the ED'
History: 'PMH: Diverticulosis, Uterine fibroids, Liver cyst'
Physical examination: 'HR: 119' (tachycardia)
Physical examination: 'Tender to deep palpation in the RUQ'
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 15.1 K/uL (ref 4.0-11.0)' (leukocytosis)
Laboratory tests: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL' and 'ALT: 22.0 IU/L' and 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 84.0 IU/L' (liver enzymes normal, biliary obstruction less likely but not excluded)
Laboratory tests: 'Lipase: 37.0 IU/L' (pancreatitis effectively excluded)
Laboratory tests: 'Blood: LG' and 'RBC: 5.0 #/hpf' in urine (hematuria raises urologic/nephrolithiasis concern)
Imaging already resulted BEFORE this decision point: (none)
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has 7 days of RUQ pain with acute worsening, tachycardia, and leukocytosis but normal liver enzymes and lipase, leaving the biliary tree, liver cyst, colon (known diverticulosis), uterine fibroids, and urinary tract all as plausible sources. No imaging has yet been obtained, creating a fundamental anatomic information gap. A contrast-enhanced CT abdomen and pelvis is the most efficient single test to simultaneously evaluate all of these structures, characterize any inflammatory process or complication, and guide definitive management.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis/biliary colic, hepatic cyst complication (hemorrhage or infection), nephrolithiasis/urologic source, gynecologic pathology (fibroid degeneration), bowel obstruction
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the RUQ pain remain undefined — no prior imaging has been performed, and the labs do not clearly point to a single etiology; the physician needs cross-sectional anatomy to identify the responsible structure.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or cholelithiasis consistent with cholecystitis/biliary colic; alternatively, a complicated hepatic cyst, ureteral stone, or colonic/diverticular pathology given the known diverticulosis and uterine fibroids
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: With RUQ pain of 7 days, tachycardia, leukocytosis (WBC 15.1), no prior imaging, and multiple competing diagnoses spanning biliary, hepatic, urologic, gynecologic, and colonic etiologies, a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the appropriate single test to survey all relevant structures simultaneously.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated gallbladder wall edema (up to 6 mm), haziness of pericholecystic fat, and a 1.3-cm radiopaque gallstone impacted in the gallbladder neck, consistent with acute cholecystitis/biliary colic.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly matched the primary expected finding of gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fat stranding, and cholelithiasis consistent with cholecystitis/biliary colic, while also confirming known diverticulosis without diverticulitis and uterine fibroids as incidental findings.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary colic with possible common bile duct stone
ensemble.information_gap: The CT shows gallbladder wall edema (up to 6 mm), pericholecystic fat haziness, and a 1.3-cm gallstone impacted in the gallbladder neck, but CT cannot reliably assess for sonographic Murphy's sign, gallbladder wall layering, pericholecystic fluid in detail, or common bile duct diameter — all of which are needed to confirm acute cholecystitis versus biliary colic and to exclude choledocholithiasis.
ensemble.expected_finding: Sonographic Murphy's sign, thickened gallbladder wall with pericholecystic fluid confirming acute cholecystitis, and/or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis from the neck stone
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The CT has already identified a gallstone impacted in the gallbladder neck with wall edema and fat stranding, making ultrasound the logical next step to confirm acute cholecystitis with real-time sonographic Murphy's sign and to evaluate the common bile duct — findings that directly guide surgical versus endoscopic management.
grounding: Prior imaging 1: 'The gallbladder is minimally distended with gallbladder wall edema evident, measuring up to 6 mm in diameter. There is also haziness of the adjacent fat.'
Prior imaging 1: 'A 1.3-cm rounded radiopaque gallstone is noted within the neck of the gallbladder.'
History: 'cramping abdominal pain localized to the RUQ and nausea and emesis'
Physical examination: 'Tender to deep palpation in the RUQ'
Physical examination: 'HR: 119' (tachycardia suggesting physiologic stress)
Laboratory tests: 'White Blood Cells: 15.1 K/uL' (leukocytosis supporting inflammatory process)
Laboratory tests: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 84.0 IU/L', 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.6 mg/dL' (normal but does not exclude partial CBD obstruction)
ensemble.reasoning: With CT demonstrating a gallstone impacted in the gallbladder neck, wall edema, and pericholecystic fat haziness alongside RUQ tenderness, leukocytosis, and tachycardia, acute cholecystitis is the leading diagnosis. Ultrasound is ordered to obtain a sonographic Murphy's sign (pathognomonic for acute cholecystitis), better characterize the gallbladder wall, assess for pericholecystic fluid, and measure the common bile duct to exclude a concomitant choledocholithiasis — information the CT cannot reliably provide and that will determine whether the patient needs cholecystectomy alone or ERCP first.

vindication.actual_finding: Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis with sludge and mild gallbladder wall thickening, but no pericholecystic fluid, a negative sonographic Murphy's sign, and a normal common bile duct measuring 0.3 cm.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The expected findings of a positive sonographic Murphy's sign, pericholecystic fluid confirming acute cholecystitis, and/or a dilated common bile duct suggesting choledocholithiasis were all absent.
